title: Data Loaders
text: data loader returns list three named components: resp, pers_tru, item_tru. simplest loader, data_existing(), just reads files:
code: data_existing <- function(resp_path, pers_path, item_path) {   list(     resp = utils::read.csv(resp_path),     pers_tru = utils::read.csv(pers_path),     item_tru = utils::read.csv(item_path)   ) }

title: Data Loaders
text: resp long-form data frame item responses three numeric columns, aligned standard used Item Response Warehouse: id (1-indexed respondent identifier), item (1-indexed item identifier), resp (response xijx_{ij}, xij∈{0,1}x_{ij} \\{0, 1\} dichotomous items). meow() converts long table respondent--item matrix , simulation loop.

title: Data Loaders
text: pers_tru data frame true person parameters. first column, id, identifies respondents; remaining columns parameters (e.g. theta). Using data frame lets add parameters — instance, extra dimensions multidimensional model — without changing framework.

title: Data Loaders
text: item_tru data frame true item parameters, item first column followed parameter columns b (difficulty) (discrimination). person parameters, can add columns freely.

title: Data Loaders
text: data loader first thing meow() calls, required arguments — may take whatever pass data_args. names avoid harness supplies modules: pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, select_seed.

title: Data Loaders
text: data_simple_1pl() generates data 1PL model standard-normal abilities difficulties: draws abilities θi∼𝒩(0,1)\theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1) difficulties bj∼𝒩(0,1)b_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1), generates responses 1PL item response function P(xij=1∣θi)=11+e−(θi−bj).P(x_{ij} = 1 \mid \theta_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\theta_i - b_j)}}.
code: data_simple_1pl <- function(N_persons = 100, N_items = 50, data_seed = 242424) {   set.seed(data_seed)   pers_tru <- data.frame(id = 1:N_persons, theta = stats::rnorm(N_persons))   item_tru <- data.frame(item = 1:N_items, b = stats::rnorm(N_items), a = 1)    theta_mat <- matrix(pers_tru$theta, N_persons, N_items)   diff_mat  <- matrix(item_tru$b, N_persons, N_items, byrow = TRUE)   p <- stats::plogis(theta_mat - diff_mat)   resp_mat <- matrix(stats::rbinom(length(p), 1, p), N_persons, N_items)    resp <- data.frame(     id = rep(seq_len(N_persons), each = N_items),     item = rep(seq_len(N_items), times = N_persons),     resp = as.vector(t(resp_mat))   )   set.seed(NULL)   list(resp = resp, pers_tru = pers_tru, item_tru = item_tru) } data <- data_simple_1pl(N_persons = 6, N_items = 4) str(data, max.level = 1) #> List of 3 #>  $ resp    :'data.frame':    24 obs. of  3 variables: #>  $ pers_tru:'data.frame':    6 obs. of  2 variables: #>  $ item_tru:'data.frame':    4 obs. of  3 variables: head(data$resp) #>   id item resp #> 1  1    1    1 #> 2  1    2    0 #> 3  1    3    1 #> 4  1    4    0 #> 5  2    1    0 #> 6  2    2    0

title: Data Loaders
text: data loader uses random number generation accepts seed reproducibility, clear seed end set.seed(NULL). Otherwise seed persists rest simulation makes downstream item selection parameter updates deterministic — prevents comparing multiple stochastic runs data set.

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: meow simulation single call meow(), repeatedly alternates item selection step parameter update step items administered. speed, simulation state represented matrices rather long data frames: Two design choices worth calling : Responses matrices. Finding items respondent yet seen single logical comparison (admin[, ] == 0) instead join growing table. positive values admin additionally encode order items administered, order-sensitive algorithms (paired-comparison updates) can use. Parameters data frames. preserves flexibility carry arbitrary person item parameters: add column pers item flows simulation untouched. rather work long data frames inside module, call meow_long(R, admin) get administered responses id/item/resp data frame, ordered respondent administration order.
code: R <- matrix(c(1, 0, 1, 1), nrow = 2) admin <- matrix(c(1L, 0L, 2L, 1L), nrow = 2) meow_long(R, admin) #>   id item resp #> 1  1    1    1 #> 2  1    2    1 #> 3  2    2    1

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: data loader sets simulation. takes arguments like (passed data_args) returns list three elements: resp — long data frame columns id, item, resp. pers_tru — true person parameters; first column id, parameters. item_tru — true item parameters; first column item, parameters. meow() turns resp response matrix R , loop starts, loader’s job produce bank potential responses ground-truth parameters. Reserved argument names loader must use ones harness supplies modules: pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, select_seed.
code: data_two_groups <- function(N_per_group = 50, N_items = 40, data_seed = 1) {   set.seed(data_seed)   N <- 2 * N_per_group   theta <- c(stats::rnorm(N_per_group, -0.5), stats::rnorm(N_per_group, 0.5))   b <- stats::rnorm(N_items)   pers_tru <- data.frame(id = seq_len(N), theta = theta)   item_tru <- data.frame(item = seq_len(N_items), b = b, a = 1)    p <- stats::plogis(outer(theta, b, "-"))   resp_mat <- matrix(stats::rbinom(length(p), 1, p), nrow = N)   resp <- data.frame(     id = rep(seq_len(N), each = N_items),     item = rep(seq_len(N_items), times = N),     resp = as.vector(t(resp_mat))   )   set.seed(NULL)   list(resp = resp, pers_tru = pers_tru, item_tru = item_tru) }  str(data_two_groups(N_per_group = 3, N_items = 4), max.level = 1) #> List of 3 #>  $ resp    :'data.frame':    24 obs. of  3 variables: #>  $ pers_tru:'data.frame':    6 obs. of  2 variables: #>  $ item_tru:'data.frame':    4 obs. of  3 variables:

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: item selection function decides item administer next. signature returns administration matrix newly chosen cells marked non-zero. need add items; harness records administration order , setting cell TRUE 1 enough. Leave previously administered cells . stop administering items (stopping rule), simply return admin unchanged — simulation halts iteration adds nothing. complete custom selector administers easiest remaining item respondent (deliberately naive rule), seeding first five items first iteration: patterns notice, avoid touching growing data frame: admin[, ] == 0 gives unadministered items respondent . rowSums(admin == 0) > 0 identifies respondents still need item. Setting admin[, pick] <- 1L administers item pick. prefer compute long data, meow_long(R, admin) always available.
code: select_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) { ... } select_easiest <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL) {   if (!any(admin != 0)) {       # first iteration: seed five items     admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L     return(admin)   }   difficulty <- item$b   for (i in which(rowSums(admin == 0) > 0)) {     remaining <- which(admin[i, ] == 0)     pick <- remaining[which.min(difficulty[remaining])]     admin[i, pick] <- 1L   }   admin }

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: parameter update function re-estimates parameters administered responses. signature returns list two elements, pers item, updated estimate data frames. administered responses R[admin != 0], respondent/item indices come (admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE). custom updater nudges ability toward simple proportion-correct heuristic leaves items fixed:
code: update_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, ...) { ... } update_pct_correct <- function(pers, item, R, admin, rate = 0.5) {   idx <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)   person <- idx[, 1]   resp <- R[idx]   pct <- tapply(resp, person, mean)   target <- stats::qlogis(pmin(pmax(pct, 0.02), 0.98)) # logit of proportion   pers$theta[as.integer(names(target))] <-     (1 - rate) * pers$theta[as.integer(names(target))] + rate * target   list(pers = pers, item = item) }

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: Custom modules plug meow() exactly like bundled ones. Extra arguments passed select_args, update_args, data_args. output object shape matter modules use — results data frame (one row per iteration, est bias column parameter), list adjacency matrices adj_mats, true parameters pers_tru item_tru. consistency lets reuse analysis plotting code across studies; see vignette("meow-viz").
code: sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_easiest,   update_fun  = update_pct_correct,   data_loader = data_two_groups,   data_args   = list(N_per_group = 25, N_items = 20),   update_args = list(rate = 0.3),   fix         = "item" )  head(sim$results[, 1:4]) #>   iter pers_theta_1_est pers_theta_2_est pers_theta_3_est #> 1    1        0.1216395       -0.1216395      -0.12163953 #> 2    2        0.2930918       -0.2930918      -0.08514767 #> 3    3        0.2914689       -0.4800515      -0.14590799 #> 4    4        0.3572759       -0.4892837      -0.25538328 #> 5    5        0.3170362       -0.4094417      -0.38671245 #> 6    6        0.2219253       -0.4082487      -0.39233825

title: Extending meow: Writing Your Own Modules
text: Data loaders return list(resp, pers_tru, item_tru) long resp. Item selection functions take (pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) return administration matrix new cells marked non-zero. Parameter update functions take (pers, item, R, admin, ...) return list(pers, item). Use admin != 0 (meow_administered()) administered mask, meow_long() want long data frames. un-administer items, implement stopping rules declining administer anything .

title: Item Selection Functions
text: Every item selection function signature receives current person estimates (pers), item estimates (item), respondent--item response matrix (R), integer administration matrix (admin), item co-exposure matrix (adj_mat). returns administration matrix newly chosen cells marked non-zero. harness records order administration, need add items. unadministered items respondent (admin[, ] == 0), respondents still needing item (rowSums(admin == 0) > 0).
code: select_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) {   # ... decide which items to administer ...   return(admin) }

title: Item Selection Functions
text: select_sequential() administers lowest-numbered remaining item respondent, producing fixed linear form.
code: select_sequential <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL) {   if (!any(admin != 0)) {     admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L  # seed the first five items     return(admin)   }   unadmin <- admin == 0   has <- which(rowSums(unadmin) > 0)   nextcol <- max.col(unadmin[has, , drop = FALSE] + 0, ties.method = "first")   admin[cbind(has, nextcol)] <- 1L   admin }

title: Item Selection Functions
text: select_random() draws one remaining item per respondent random. accepts select_seed reproducibility, clears use.

title: Item Selection Functions
text: select_max_info() administers remaining item greatest 2PL Fisher information, (θ)=a2P(θ)(1−P(θ))(\theta) = ^2 P(\theta)(1 - P(\theta)), evaluated respondent’s current ability estimate. information every respondent--item combination computed single matrix, maximum taken per row.

title: Item Selection Functions
text: select_max_dist() select_max_dist_enhanced() treat item pool network whose edge weights derived co-exposure matrix adj_mat. administer item farthest (shortest-path distance) items respondent already seen, breaking ties maximum information. See vignette("network-item-selection").

title: Item Selection Functions
text: custom selector need follow signature return updated admin. administer item whose difficulty closest respondent’s current ability — simple “target trait” rule. Use simulation passing select_fun:
code: select_targeted <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL) {   if (!any(admin != 0)) {     admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L     return(admin)   }   for (i in which(rowSums(admin == 0) > 0)) {     remaining <- which(admin[i, ] == 0)     gap <- abs(item$b[remaining] - pers$theta[i])     admin[i, remaining[which.min(gap)]] <- 1L   }   admin } sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_targeted,   update_fun  = update_theta_mle,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 50, N_items = 30),   fix         = "item" ) nrow(sim$results) #> [1] 26

title: Item Selection Functions
text: Handle first iteration (!(admin != 0)) seed initial set items. Never un-administer item already given. Implement stopping rules returning admin unchanged respondent receive items. Prefer matrix operations per-row loops possible; use meow_long() long data genuinely convenient.

title: Implementing the Maths Garden Update Algorithm
text: algorithm updates abilities (θ\theta) difficulties (bb) prediction errors: θjnew=θj+Kθ∑∈Ij(Sij−E(Sij)),binew=bi+Kb∑j∈Ji(E(Sij)−Sij),\theta_j^{new} = \theta_j + K_\theta \sum_{\I_j} (S_{ij} - E(S_{ij})), \qquad b_i^{new} = b_i + K_b \sum_{j \J_i} (E(S_{ij}) - S_{ij}), Sij∈{0,1}S_{ij} \\{0, 1\} observed response, IjI_j JiJ_i items answered person jj people answered item ii, KθK_\theta, KbK_b learning rates. expected response follows Rasch (1PL) model, E(Sij)=11+e−(θj−bi).E(S_{ij}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\theta_j - b_i)}}. Intuitively, performing better expected raises ability estimate; item answered correctly often expected becomes easier.

title: Implementing the Maths Garden Update Algorithm
text: update_maths_garden() follows parameter update contract (vignette("parameter-update")): reads administered responses matrix state returns updated pers item data frames. prediction errors aggregated per respondent per item tapply() rather explicit loops:
code: update_maths_garden <- function(pers, item, R, admin, K_theta = 0.1, K_b = 0.1) {   idx    <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)   person <- idx[, 1]   itm    <- idx[, 2]   resp   <- R[idx]    E_Sij <- stats::plogis(pers$theta[person] - item$b[itm])    dtheta <- tapply(resp - E_Sij, person, sum)   pers$theta[as.integer(names(dtheta))] <-     pers$theta[as.integer(names(dtheta))] + K_theta * dtheta    db <- tapply(E_Sij - resp, itm, sum)   item$b[as.integer(names(db))] <-     item$b[as.integer(names(db))] + K_b * db    list(pers = pers, item = item) }

title: Implementing the Maths Garden Update Algorithm
text: Learning rates passed update_args:
code: sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_max_info,   update_fun  = update_maths_garden,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 100, N_items = 50),   update_args = list(K_theta = 0.05, K_b = 0.05) ) head(sim$results[, 1:3]) #>   iter pers_theta_1_est pers_theta_2_est #> 1    1        0.1250000       -0.1250000 #> 2    2        0.2719033       -0.2595475 #> 3    3        0.3638822       -0.3739552 #> 4    4        0.4624840       -0.4569931 #> 5    5        0.5127578       -0.5627726 #> 6    6        0.5456892       -0.6114085

title: Implementing the Maths Garden Update Algorithm
text: update functions ordinary R functions, variations easy. following adds adaptive learning rates (shrinking respondent answers items) bounds estimates, staying within matrix contract:
code: update_maths_garden_adaptive <- function(pers, item, R, admin,                                          base_K = 0.1, decay = 0.05,                                          bounds = c(-4, 4)) {   idx    <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)   person <- idx[, 1]   itm    <- idx[, 2]   resp   <- R[idx]   E_Sij  <- stats::plogis(pers$theta[person] - item$b[itm])    n_person <- tapply(resp, person, length)   err_p    <- tapply(resp - E_Sij, person, sum)   who_p    <- as.integer(names(err_p))   K_p      <- base_K / (1 + n_person * decay)   pers$theta[who_p] <- pers$theta[who_p] + K_p * err_p   pers$theta <- pmin(pmax(pers$theta, bounds[1]), bounds[2])    n_item <- tapply(resp, itm, length)   err_i  <- tapply(E_Sij - resp, itm, sum)   who_i  <- as.integer(names(err_i))   K_i    <- base_K / (1 + n_item * decay)   item$b[who_i] <- item$b[who_i] + K_i * err_i   item$b <- pmin(pmax(item$b, bounds[1]), bounds[2])    list(pers = pers, item = item) }

title: Implementing the Maths Garden Update Algorithm
text: Start modest learning rates (Kθ=Kb=0.1K_\theta = K_b = 0.1) check estimates stabilize across iterations. Large learning rates can make estimates oscillate; bounding estimates helps. algorithm assumes Rasch model; data need discrimination parameters, consider MLE updater 2PL extension.

title: Visualizing Item Exposure Control Methods
text: baseline, use built-select_max_info() selector 1PL data MLE ability updates: results data frame one row per iteration, estimate bias column every parameter, can track estimates evolve test.
code: out_none <- meow(   select_fun = select_max_info,   update_fun = update_theta_mle,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   init = NULL,   fix = "item" )

title: Visualizing Item Exposure Control Methods
text: Individual bias trajectories also informative:
code: results_none <- out_none$results |>   mutate(control = "None") results_none |>   select(iter, control, starts_with("pers_")) |>   select(iter, control, ends_with("_bias")) |>   pivot_longer(ends_with("_bias"), names_to = "person", values_to = "bias") |>   group_by(iter, control) |>   summarize(rmse = sqrt(mean(bias^2)), .groups = "drop") |>   ggplot(aes(x = iter, y = rmse, color = control)) +   geom_line() +   labs(x = "Number of Items Delivered", y = "RMSE for Person Ability",        color = "Exposure Controls") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") results_none |>   select(iter, control, starts_with("pers_")) |>   select(iter, control, ends_with("_bias")) |>   pivot_longer(ends_with("_bias"), names_to = "person", values_to = "bias") |>   ggplot(aes(x = iter, y = bias, color = control, group = person)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +   labs(x = "Number of Items Delivered", y = "Bias in Estimated Person Ability",        color = "Exposure Controls") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Visualizing Item Exposure Control Methods
text: add simple exposure control, write custom selector. diagonal adjacency matrix holds item’s exposure count, convert exposure rate refuse administer items whose rate exceeds r_max, choosing informative item among remain. Pass non-default exposure rate select_args: can compare RMSE across two methods:
code: select_rest <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL, r_max = 0.025) {   if (!any(admin != 0)) {     admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L     return(admin)   }   # exposure rate for every item   exposures <- diag(adj_mat)   r_obs <- exposures / sum(exposures)   allowed <- which(r_obs < r_max)    # 2PL information for every respondent-item combination   lin <- sweep(outer(pers$theta, item$b, "-"), 2, item$a, "*")   P <- stats::plogis(lin)   info <- sweep(P * (1 - P), 2, item$a^2, "*")    for (i in which(rowSums(admin == 0) > 0)) {     candidates <- intersect(which(admin[i, ] == 0), allowed)     if (length(candidates) == 0) next            # all permissible items used up     admin[i, candidates[which.max(info[i, candidates])]] <- 1L   }   admin } out_rest <- meow(   select_fun = select_rest,   update_fun = update_theta_mle,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   init = NULL,   fix = "item",   select_args = list(r_max = 0.02) ) results_rest <- out_rest$results |>   mutate(control = "Restricted")  results <- bind_rows(results_none, results_rest)  results |>   select(iter, control, starts_with("pers_")) |>   select(iter, control, ends_with("_bias")) |>   pivot_longer(ends_with("_bias"), names_to = "person", values_to = "bias") |>   group_by(iter, control) |>   summarize(rmse = sqrt(mean(bias^2)), .groups = "drop") |>   ggplot(aes(x = iter, y = rmse, color = control)) +   geom_line() +   labs(x = "Number of Items Delivered", y = "RMSE for Person Ability",        color = "Exposure Controls") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") results |>   select(iter, control, starts_with("pers_")) |>   select(iter, control, ends_with("_bias")) |>   pivot_longer(ends_with("_bias"), names_to = "person", values_to = "bias") |>   filter(iter <= 20) |>   ggplot(aes(x = iter, y = bias, color = control, group = person)) +   geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +   facet_grid(control ~ .) +   labs(x = "Number of Items Delivered", y = "Bias in Estimated Person Ability",        color = "Exposure Controls") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Visualizing Item Exposure Control Methods
text: list adjacency matrices returned adj_mats makes easy build dynamic network visualizations item utilization statnet ndtv.
code: library(statnet) library(ndtv)  rest_nets <- lapply(out_rest$adj_mats, network) dyn_rest <- networkDynamic(network.list = rest_nets)  render.d3movie(   dyn_rest,   usearrows = FALSE,   main = "Maximum Fisher Information Item Selection",   vertex.cex = abs(out_rest$item_tru$b),   vertex.col = ifelse(out_rest$item_tru$b < 0, "dodgerblue", "tomato") )

title: Network-Based Item Selection
text: item pool weighted graph: nodes items, edge weights derive co-exposure matrix adj_mat (entry (,j)(, j) number respondents seen items). Floyd–Warshall algorithm (Rfast::floyd()) turns edge-weight matrix WW -pairs shortest-path distance matrix DD. respondent, distance candidate item set administered items minimum distance , farthest candidate administered (ties broken maximum information).

title: Network-Based Item Selection
text: edge-weight function maps co-exposure counts graph weights, choice shapes behavior. bundled unchanged: Inverse / negative log / exponential: co-responses give smaller weights, frequently co-administered items “closer” algorithm spreads exposure across dissimilar items. Linear: co-responses give larger weights, inverting logic. Power: beta < 1 dampens beta > 1 amplifies effect high co-response counts.
code: edge_weight_inverse(adj_mat, alpha = 1)              # 1 / (adj_mat + alpha) edge_weight_negative_log(adj_mat, alpha = 1)         # -log(adj_mat + alpha) edge_weight_linear(adj_mat, max_co_responses = NULL) # adj_mat / max(adj_mat) edge_weight_power(adj_mat, beta = 0.5, alpha = 1)    # (adj_mat + alpha)^beta edge_weight_exponential(adj_mat, lambda = 0.1)       # exp(-lambda*(adj_mat+alpha))

title: Network-Based Item Selection
text: select_max_dist() follows item selection contract (vignette("item-selection")): works matrix administration state returns updated admin. distance matrix computed, per-item distances obtained Rfast::colMins() rather row-wise data-frame operation: select_max_dist_enhanced() identical except edge weights come user-supplied edge_weight_fun applied adj_mat Rfast::floyd().
code: select_max_dist <- function(pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat = NULL, n_candidates = 1) {   if (!any(admin != 0)) {     admin[, seq_len(min(5, ncol(admin)))] <- 1L     # seed five items     return(admin)   }   dist_mat <- Rfast::floyd(1 / adj_mat)              # all-pairs shortest paths   info <- {                                          # 2PL information matrix     lin <- sweep(outer(pers$theta, item$b, "-"), 2, item$a, "*")     P <- stats::plogis(lin); sweep(P * (1 - P), 2, item$a^2, "*")   }   for (i in which(rowSums(admin == 0) > 0)) {     administered <- which(admin[i, ] != 0)     candidates   <- which(admin[i, ] == 0)     sub <- dist_mat[administered, candidates, drop = FALSE]     cand_dist <- if (length(administered) == 1L) sub[1, ] else Rfast::colMins(sub, value = TRUE)     pool <- candidates[cand_dist >= max(cand_dist)]  # farthest items     admin[i, pool[which.max(info[i, pool])]] <- 1L   # tie-break by information   }   admin }

title: Network-Based Item Selection
text: small runnable example default inverse weights: Swap different edge-weight function select_max_dist_enhanced():
code: sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_max_dist,   update_fun  = update_theta_mle,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 50, N_items = 30),   select_args = list(n_candidates = 3),   fix         = "item" ) nrow(sim$results) #> [1] 26 # Power transformation with beta = 0.3 meow(   select_fun  = select_max_dist_enhanced,   update_fun  = update_theta_mle,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 100, N_items = 50),   select_args = list(     n_candidates = 3,     edge_weight_fun = edge_weight_power,     edge_weight_args = list(beta = 0.3, alpha = 1)   ),   fix = "item" )

title: Network-Based Item Selection
text: Rfast::floyd() O(n3)O(n^3) number items run iteration, network selection expensive select_max_info(). Experiment n_candidates (1–5) trade exposure control measurement efficiency, compare simpler selectors baseline.

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: Every parameter update function signature receives current estimates (pers, item), response matrix R, administration matrix admin, returns list updated pers item data frames. administered responses obtained matrix state: , equivalently, long data frame meow_long(R, admin).
code: update_fun <- function(pers, item, R, admin, ...) {   # ... re-estimate parameters ...   list(pers = updated_pers, item = updated_item) } idx    <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE) person <- idx[, 1] item_j <- idx[, 2] resp   <- R[idx]

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: update_theta_mle() treats item parameters fixed finds respondent’s 2PL maximum likelihood ability estimate, constrained [−4,4][-4, 4]. log-likelihood fully vectorized administered responses:
code: loglik <- function(theta) {   p <- stats::plogis(item$a[item_j] * (theta[person] - item$b[item_j]))   sum(resp * log(p) + (1 - resp) * log(1 - p)) } est <- stats::optim(pers$theta, loglik, lower = -4, upper = 4,                     method = "L-BFGS-B", control = list(fnscale = -1))

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: update_maths_garden() updates abilities difficulties --fly Elo rule Klinkenberg, Straatemeier, van der Maas (2011): θ̂j=θj+Kθ∑(Sij−E(Sij)),b̂=bi+Kb∑j(E(Sij)−Sij).\hat\theta_j = \theta_j + K_\theta \sum_i (S_{ij} - E(S_{ij})), \qquad \hat b_i = b_i + K_b \sum_j (E(S_{ij}) - S_{ij}). See vignette("maths-garden-update").

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: update_prowise_learn() updates abilities rule, updates item difficulties paired comparisons consecutively administered items, controls rating drift (Vermeiren et al., 2025). See vignette("prowise-learn-update").

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: updater shrinks ability estimate toward mean administered responses (crude illustrative rule) leaves items unchanged.
code: update_shrink <- function(pers, item, R, admin, weight = 0.5) {   idx <- which(admin != 0, arr.ind = TRUE)   person <- idx[, 1]   resp <- R[idx]   score <- tapply(resp, person, mean)   target <- stats::qlogis(pmin(pmax(score, 0.02), 0.98))   who <- as.integer(names(target))   pers$theta[who] <- (1 - weight) * pers$theta[who] + weight * target   list(pers = pers, item = item) }  sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_max_info,   update_fun  = update_shrink,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 50, N_items = 30),   update_args = list(weight = 0.3),   fix         = "item" ) nrow(sim$results) #> [1] 26

title: Parameter Update Functions
text: Return list(pers, item) — data frames, even one unchanged. Bound estimates sensible range avoid divergence. Vectorize administered responses (tapply(), matrix indexing) rather looping respondents items. Respect administration order matters: meow_long() returns responses ordered respondent administration order, paired-comparison methods rely .

title: Implementing the Prowise Learn Update Algorithm
text: Abilities updated exactly Maths Garden: θjnew=θj+Kθ∑∈Ij(Sij−E(Sij)).\theta_j^{new} = \theta_j + K_\theta \sum_{\I_j} (S_{ij} - E(S_{ij})). Item difficulties, however, updated consecutive pairs items administered respondent. pair (previous item, current item), κ=0.5(Kb(Snow−Enow)−Kb(Sprev−Eprev)),bnow+=κ,bprev−=κ.\kappa = 0.5\,\big(K_b (S_{now} - E_{now}) - K_b (S_{prev} - E_{prev})\big), \qquad b_{now} \mathrel{+}= \kappa, \quad b_{prev} \mathrel{-}= \kappa. pair adds +κ+\kappa one item −κ-\kappa , total difficulty mass conserved, items keep relative positions drift en masse. Expected responses use Rasch model, E(Sij)=1/(1+e−(θj−bi))E(S_{ij}) = 1 / (1 + e^{-(\theta_j - b_i)}).

title: Implementing the Prowise Learn Update Algorithm
text: Paired updates inherently order dependent, update_prowise_learn() uses meow_long(R, admin), returns administered responses ordered respondent administration order. Consecutive within-respondent rows form pairs; per-item contributions aggregated tapply():
code: update_prowise_learn <- function(pers, item, R, admin, K_theta = 0.1, K_b = 0.1) {   long  <- meow_long(R, admin)   E_Sij <- stats::plogis(pers$theta[long$id] - item$b[long$item])    # ability update (as in Maths Garden)   dtheta <- tapply(long$resp - E_Sij, long$id, sum)   pers$theta[as.integer(names(dtheta))] <-     pers$theta[as.integer(names(dtheta))] + K_theta * dtheta    # paired item updates over consecutive administrations   n <- nrow(long)   if (n >= 2) {     nxt <- 2:n; prv <- 1:(n - 1)     pair <- which(long$id[nxt] == long$id[prv])     if (length(pair) > 0) {       now <- nxt[pair]; pre <- prv[pair]       kappa <- 0.5 * (K_b * (long$resp[now] - E_Sij[now]) -                       K_b * (long$resp[pre] - E_Sij[pre]))       add_now <- tapply(kappa,  long$item[now], sum)       add_pre <- tapply(-kappa, long$item[pre], sum)       item$b[as.integer(names(add_now))] <- item$b[as.integer(names(add_now))] + add_now       item$b[as.integer(names(add_pre))] <- item$b[as.integer(names(add_pre))] + add_pre     }   }   list(pers = pers, item = item) }

title: Implementing the Prowise Learn Update Algorithm
code: sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_max_info,   update_fun  = update_prowise_learn,   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,   data_args   = list(N_persons = 100, N_items = 50),   update_args = list(K_theta = 0.05, K_b = 0.05) ) head(sim$results[, 1:3]) #>   iter pers_theta_1_est pers_theta_2_est #> 1    1        0.1250000       -0.1250000 #> 2    2        0.2656826       -0.2657371 #> 3    3        0.3692123       -0.3715956 #> 4    4        0.4806109       -0.4711769 #> 5    5        0.5559021       -0.6101828 #> 6    6        0.6200887       -0.7172594

title: Implementing the Prowise Learn Update Algorithm
text: Paired updates require respondents answer least two items, item difficulties begin move administration way. Effectiveness depends administration order; exactly matrix admin carries order administration. Maths Garden, keep learning rates modest consider bounding estimates stability.

code: @Manual{,   title = {meow: A Unified Framework for Conducting Simulations of Computer Adaptive Testing Algorithms in R},   author = {Klint Kanopka and Sophia Deng},   year = {2025},   note = {R package},   url = {http://klintkanopka.com/meow/}, }

what: Unified Framework for Computer Adaptive Testing Simulations
title: Unified Framework for Computer Adaptive Testing Simulations
text: meow package conducting simulations computer adaptive testing (CAT). pitch meow framework facilitates reproducible comparisons different combinations data generating processes (DGPs), item selection algorithms, parameter update algorithms. functionalizing components treating modular use core simulation harness produces consistent outputs ggplot2-based visualization tools. goal expose structure component functions user, allowing implement custom DGPs, selection algorithms, update algorithms. Users also encouraged contribute function modules associated research projects, facilitating community interaction.

title: Unified Framework for Computer Adaptive Testing Simulations
text: Interested users can install using:
code: devtools::install_github("klintkanopka/meow")

title: Unified Framework for Computer Adaptive Testing Simulations
text: simulation single call meow(), takes item selection function, parameter update function, data loader: sim$results tidy data frame (one row per iteration, est/bias columns per parameter) plugs directly ggplot2. sim$adj_mats holds item co-exposure adjacency matrices.
code: library(meow)  sim <- meow(   select_fun  = select_max_info,    # item selection algorithm   update_fun  = update_theta_mle,   # parameter update algorithm   data_loader = data_simple_1pl,    # data generating process   data_args   = list(N_persons = 100, N_items = 50),   fix         = "item"              # treat item parameters as known )  head(sim$results)   # per-iteration estimates and bias

title: Unified Framework for Computer Adaptive Testing Simulations
text: real value meow swapping algorithms. Internally simulation state matrix-based speed: item selection parameter update functions receive respondent--item response matrix R integer administration matrix admin, person/item parameters stay data frames can add arbitrary columns. See vignette("extending-meow") full module contracts, use meow_long() work long data frames instead.

title: Load data from existing files — data_existing

File three columns, id contains numeric respondent identifier, item contains numeric item identifier, resp contains item response. sure form item response comports parameter update functions choose use. pers_path file path wide form .csv file contains true person parameter values, one person per row. Include person index column, named id. Default column name unidimensional person ability theta item_path file path wide form .csv file contains true item parameter values, one item per row. Include item index column, named item. Default column names difficulty b default column name discrimination ,

what: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
text: meow() core function simulation framework. exists help users compare efficiency tradeoffs across different item selection algorithms, parameter update algorithms, data generating processes. takes arguments item selection function, parameter update function, data loader function uses carry simulation full CAT administration. Default behavior proceed items administered. internal simulation logic stops soon iteration administers new items, early stopping conditions implemented within item selection function (declining administer items).

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
code: meow(   select_fun,   update_fun,   data_loader,   select_args = list(),   update_args = list(),   data_args = list(),   init = NULL,   fix = "none",   keep_adj_mats = TRUE )

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
text: select_fun function specifies item selection algorithm. update_fun function specifies parameter update algorithm. data_loader function specifies data generating process. select_args named list arguments passed select_fun. update_args named list arguments passed update_fun. data_args named list arguments passed data_loader. init list initialization values estimated person item parameters. Accepts named list two entries, pers item, giving initial estimated parameter data frames. Defaults NULL, initializes estimated parameters zero. fix estimated parameters treat fixed true values. One none (default), pers, item, . keep_adj_mats Logical; TRUE (default) adjacency matrix stored every iteration. FALSE, final adjacency matrix retained, saves memory large item pools long simulations.

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
text: list four named entities. results data frame one row per iteration simulation. contains iter column iteration number two columns per person item parameter, one estimated parameter one bias estimate. adj_mats list item-item adjacency matrices, one per iteration (, keep_adj_mats = FALSE, single-element list final matrix); edge weights count number respondents administered pair items. pers_tru item_tru true person item parameter data frames.

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
text: speed, meow() represents responses matrices rather long data frames. Two matrices, one row per respondent one column per item, passed user-supplied modules: R — (potential) response every respondent every item. produced long resp data frame returned data loader. admin — integer administration matrix. entry 0 means item administered respondent; positive entry means , value encodes order administration. Use admin != 0 (meow_administered()) administered mask. Person item parameters kept data frames (pers item), identifier column (id item, respectively) followed one column per parameter, users retain flexibility add arbitrary parameters.

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow
text: item selection function receives pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat (plus select_args) returns administration matrix newly selected cells marked non-zero. harness stamps order administration, function need set newly selected cells positive value (TRUE) leaving previously administered cells unchanged. parameter update function receives pers, item, R, admin (plus update_args) returns list updated pers item data frames. Module authors prefer long data frames can convert meow_long().

title: Conduct a full CAT simulation. — meow

title: meow 1.0.0
text: First public release.

title: meow 1.0.0
text: meow() runs full CAT administration simulation three swappable modules: data loader, item selection function, parameter update function. Item selection functions take (pers, item, R, admin, adj_mat, ...) return updated admin matrix newly administered cells marked non-zero. Parameter update functions take (pers, item, R, admin, ...) return list updated pers item data frames. Bundled data loaders (data_existing(), data_simple_1pl()), item selectors (select_sequential(), select_random(), select_max_info(), select_max_dist(), select_max_dist_enhanced()), parameter updaters (update_theta_mle(), update_maths_garden(), update_prowise_learn()). Helpers module authors: meow_long() converts matrix state long (id, item, resp) data frame, meow_administered() returns logical mask administered items, construct_adj_mat() builds item co-exposure matrix. meow() accepts keep_adj_mats argument; set FALSE retain final adjacency matrix save memory large long simulations. Vignettes cover getting started, module type, bundled algorithms, dedicated “Extending meow” guide writing modules.
